terms.definition: A young Native American boy from the poem who grew up in a forest and learned the ways of birds and animals.
terms.exampleUsage: Hiawatha learned the language of every bird in the forest.

terms.definition: Hiawatha's old grandmother who raised him and taught him about the world around him.
terms.exampleUsage: Old Nokomis told Hiawatha stories about the stars and the moon.

terms.definition: The first language that a person learns as a baby from their parents and family.
terms.exampleUsage: No matter how many languages you learn, your mother tongue is always special.

terms.definition: A person who has studied a lot and has great knowledge, especially about books and learning.
terms.exampleUsage: The scholar could speak many languages fluently.

terms.definition: A word used in place of a noun to avoid repeating the same name. Examples: he, she, it, they, we, I, you.
terms.exampleUsage: Instead of saying 'Birbal was smart, Birbal solved the puzzle,' we say 'Birbal was smart, he solved the puzzle.'

terms.definition: A shelter or home, especially one made by animals. In the poem, beavers build their lodges near water.
terms.exampleUsage: Hiawatha knew where the beavers built their lodges.

terms.definition: A person who serves or attends a king or emperor in the royal court.
terms.exampleUsage: The courtiers in Akbar's court tried to find out the scholar's mother tongue.

terms.definition: Speaking a language smoothly and easily, without stopping or making mistakes.
terms.exampleUsage: The pandit spoke every language so fluently that nobody could guess his mother tongue.

terms.definition: An animal, especially a large or wild one. In the poem, Hiawatha learned the names of all the beasts.
terms.exampleUsage: Hiawatha knew the names of all the beasts in the forest.

terms.definition: The ability to think clearly and make good decisions. Being wise means knowing the right thing to do.
terms.exampleUsage: Birbal used his wisdom to solve the puzzle that no one else could.
